options.A: Wait until the student has stable housing before beginning evaluation
options.B: Proceed with evaluation under IDEA regardless of housing status; remove barriers to participation (flexible scheduling, transportation if needed)
options.C: Assume homelessness explains all academic and behavioral difficulties
options.D: Refer the student to social services instead of special education

questions.explanation: Homelessness does not exempt students from special education protections; schools must remove barriers to participation. Option A delays needed evaluation. Option C avoids assessment of potential disability. Option D is not mutually exclusive with special education.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist is evaluating an ELL student for a learning disability. The evaluation should:

options.A: Be conducted entirely in English since that is the school language
options.B: Include assessment in both the home language and English; include bilingual personnel or interpreters; examine performance in both languages
options.C: Use only nonverbal tests to avoid language confounds
options.D: Be delayed until the student is proficient in English

questions.explanation: Comprehensive assessment of ELL students includes bilingual evaluation to separate language difference from disorder. Option A prevents understanding true functioning. Option C limits assessment. Option D delays needed evaluation.

questions.question_stem: Regarding the school-to-prison pipeline, school psychologists should be aware that:

options.A: Their role in discipline referral and special education placement can either perpetuate or help prevent the pipeline
options.B: The pipeline is unrelated to school special education decisions
options.C: Schools have no responsibility in addressing this issue
options.D: Law enforcement, not schools, causes involvement in the criminal justice system

questions.explanation: School psychologists' decisions about discipline, behavioral interventions, and special education affect involvement in criminal justice. Awareness of bias and advocacy for equity-focused practices can reduce pipeline risk. Option B ignores the school's role. Options C and D deny responsibility.

questions.question_stem: An IEP team is considering a more restrictive placement for a student with a disability. Under IDEA's LRE requirement, the team must:

options.A: Automatically place the student in the least restrictive setting (general education)
options.B: Consider whether less restrictive options with supplementary supports could meet the student's needs before moving to more restrictive settings
options.C: Focus on the student's disability category when determining placement
options.D: Place all students with disabilities in separate settings to protect non-disabled peers

questions.explanation: LRE requires considering a continuum with general education first and moving to more restrictive settings only if supplementary supports don't work. Option A ignores that some students need more restrictive settings. Option C ignores individualized placement decisions. Option D is incorrect and harmful.

questions.question_stem: When serving gifted students from underrepresented groups, the school should:

options.A: Lower curriculum rigor to be more culturally accessible
options.B: Identify gifted characteristics through diverse methods; provide culturally sustaining curriculum; ensure advanced opportunities are not limited by bias
options.C: Wait until students demonstrate achievement in English before considering for gifted services
options.D: Provide only career/technical education pathways

questions.explanation: Underrepresented gifted students need diverse identification, advanced curriculum, and culturally relevant delivery. Option A lowers standards inappropriately. Option C delays identification. Option D limits opportunities.

questions.question_stem: A student with a disability who is in foster care is entitled to:

options.A: Automatic placement in special education regardless of actual need
options.B: IDEA protections; the school must also coordinate with child welfare and address trauma-informed practices in the IEP
options.C: Limited special education services due to placement instability
options.D: Special education only if the biological parent requests it

questions.explanation: Students in foster care have full IDEA protections plus additional coordination needs and trauma consideration. Option A provides services unnecessarily. Option C reduces access. Option D ignores school responsibility.

questions.question_stem: A district notices that students from a particular socioeconomic background are overrepresented in intellectual disability (ID) identifications. What should be investigated?

options.A: Whether the assessment instruments are biased or use culturally loaded items
options.B: Whether students are being misidentified due to assessment bias, language differences, or low SES affecting test performance
options.C: Whether students actually have more cases of ID based on SES
options.D: Whether IQ testing is the only measure of ID

questions.explanation: Over-representation in ID suggests assessment bias or misidentification. Option A is partially correct but less comprehensive. Option C assumes disparity reflects reality. Option D, while valid, doesn't directly address over-representation.

questions.question_stem: Extended School Year (ESY) services should be considered for students:

options.A: Only in academic disability categories
options.B: With significant needs who would regress without services over the summer break; decisions should be individualized and consider the student's disability and needs
options.C: Based on age only (e.g., all students ages 5-8)
options.D: Never, as students need summer break time

questions.explanation: ESY is individualized, considering regression and whether the student has significant needs. Option A incorrectly limits to academic categories. Option C uses age as sole criterion. Option D denies needed services.

questions.question_stem: When considering special education eligibility, a school must ensure that:

options.A: Minority students are rarely identified for special education to avoid disparities
options.B: Assessment is nondiscriminatory, culturally appropriate, and involves comprehensive evaluation from multiple sources before determining eligibility
options.C: All students who struggle academically are placed in special education
options.D: The student's race/ethnicity influences the disability determination

questions.explanation:
IDEA requires nondiscriminatory, comprehensive evaluation. This protects against both over- and underidentification. Option A creates underrepresentation. Option C over-identifies. Option D is inappropriate.

---

questions.question_stem: A student discloses to the school psychologist that their parent is hitting them. The psychologist must:

options.A: Keep this completely confidential with no exceptions
options.B: Speak with the parent first before reporting to child protective services
options.C: Make a mandatory report to child protective services/law enforcement as required by law
options.D: Discuss the situation with the school principal and wait for the principal to decide

questions.explanation: School psychologists are mandated reporters; child abuse must be reported regardless of confidentiality considerations. Option A wrongly treats confidentiality as absolute. Option B creates delay and risk. Option D inappropriately defers the mandated reporter responsibility.

questions.question_stem: According to NASP Principles for Professional Ethics (2020), the four broad themes are:

options.A: Competence, Confidentiality, Compliance, and Consultation
options.B: Beneficence and Nonmaleficence, Justice and Fairness, Respect for Dignity and Rights, and Professional Responsibility
options.C: Assessment, Intervention, Advocacy, and Organization
options.D: Individual, Family, School, and Community

questions.explanation: These are the four foundational ethical principles in NASP's 2020 ethics framework. Options A, C, and D are incorrect groupings.

questions.question_stem: A parent asks to see the raw test data from their child's psychological evaluation. Under FERPA and ethical guidelines, the school psychologist should:

options.A: Provide the raw data directly to the parent
options.B: Provide an interpretation of the data; raw data can be provided to a qualified professional designated by the parent
options.C: Refuse to provide any test information due to copyright
options.D: Provide the data only with written consent from the test publisher

questions.explanation: Raw test data should be interpreted for parents; they can request it go to a qualified professional, protecting both the student and test security. Option A violates test security. Option C over-restricts access. Option D incorrectly requires publisher consent.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist is asked to conduct a third-party evaluation (ordered by a parent's attorney) at the school during the school day. Ethical considerations include:

options.A: The school psychologist can automatically refuse due to work obligations
options.B: Clarifying who will pay for the evaluation, ensuring informed consent about the limited confidentiality (it may be used in legal proceedings), and considering impact on the student's school day
options.C: Conducting the evaluation without student assent since the parent consented
options.D: Using school test materials without considering copyright or test security

questions.explanation: Third-party evaluations require clear agreements about confidentiality, consent, compensation, and logistics. Option A oversimplifies. Option C ignores student assent. Option D violates test protocols.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist has a family member who is a teacher in the same school. This creates a dual relationship that:

options.A: Must be automatically prohibited regardless of circumstances
options.B: Can be managed by maintaining professional boundaries, ensuring neither person supervises the other in conflict areas, and being transparent about the relationship
options.C: Is ethical as long as the individuals are honest about it
options.D: Is acceptable only if they teach different grades

questions.explanation: Some dual relationships are unavoidable in school settings; they require careful management, boundaries, and transparency. Option A is overly rigid. Options C and D imply management is automatic without intentional steps.

questions.question_stem: A student is 17 years old and wants a psychological evaluation but is reluctant to have parents involved. The school psychologist should:

options.A: Proceed with the evaluation without parental knowledge
options.B: Require full parental consent and tell the parent about the student's reluctance
options.C: Explain to the student that parental consent is required; discuss the student's concerns and explore whether the parent can be involved in a way that respects the student's concerns
options.D: Refuse to conduct any evaluation

questions.explanation: Even with minors, school psychologists balance parental rights (they provide legal consent) with the student's assent and confidentiality concerns. Option A bypasses consent. Option B dismisses the student's concerns. Option D is unnecessary.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist suspects a colleague is providing services outside their scope of competence. The ethical obligation is to:

options.A: Ignore the situation to avoid conflict
options.B: Speak privately with the colleague, expressing concern; if unresolved, escalate to administration or appropriate oversight body
options.C: Report the colleague to licensure boards immediately without discussion
options.D: Inform parents of the concern

questions.explanation: Addressing concerns through appropriate channels—first directly with the colleague, then through supervision—is the ethical approach. Option A ignores an ethical obligation. Option C skips internal resolution steps. Option D inappropriately involves parents.

questions.question_stem: According to ethical standards for documentation, a school psychologist's case notes should:

options.A: Include only test scores and diagnosis
options.B: Be detailed enough to document the basis for decisions, recommendations, and interventions; be objective and factual; and avoid personal opinions
options.C: Be written in vague terms to protect student privacy
options.D: Include speculation about family dynamics or student behavior outside the assessment context

questions.explanation: Good documentation supports decision-making, protects the student, and defends the school psychologist legally. Option A is too limited. Option C reduces usability. Option D introduces unsubstantiated information.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist becomes aware they made an error in interpreting a student's test results that affects the IEP decision. The ethical obligation is to:

options.A: Hope no one notices and continue as planned
options.B: Immediately correct the error, inform the team, revise the interpretation, and communicate with the family
options.C: Tell only the principal to avoid embarrassing the team
options.D: Wait until the next annual review to correct it

questions.explanation: Beneficence requires correcting errors that affect students. Transparency and timely correction are ethical obligations. Options A, C, and D delay correcting an error that impacts the student.

questions.question_stem: Informed consent for school psychological services requires:

options.A: Only the parent's signature on a form
options.B: The parent and student (when age-appropriate) understanding what services will be provided, what will happen with information, and the right to refuse; consent must be based on true understanding
options.C: No consent since schools have blanket authority
options.D: Consent only for evaluations, not for interventions

questions.explanation: Informed consent requires actual understanding and voluntary agreement. Assent (agreement from the student when old enough) complements consent. Option A is pro forma without understanding. Option C ignores consent obligations. Option D creates false categories.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist is consulted by a teacher about a student's behavior but has limited time. The ethical approach is to:

options.A: Provide quick advice without full assessment information
options.B: Acknowledge the limits of consultation without full assessment; offer to conduct a more thorough assessment or refer to a colleague with more time
options.C: Give the teacher an evidence-based intervention to try
options.D: Defer the consultation to another staff member without follow-up

questions.explanation: Competence boundaries require being honest about limitations and offering appropriate alternatives. Option A provides advice without adequate information. Option C may be appropriate but doesn't address the time limitation. Option D abdicates responsibility.

questions.question_stem: A parent offers to trade childcare services for the school psychologist's assessment of their child. The school psychologist should:

options.A: Accept the barter arrangement to help the family
options.B: Decline the barter; explain that the school has payment mechanisms and bartering can create boundary issues
options.C: Accept the barter if the school administrator approves
options.D: Offer to trade services with a reduced fee instead

questions.explanation: NASP ethics prohibit bartering because it creates boundary issues and conflicts of interest. Option A violates ethics standards. Option C doesn't change the ethical problem. Option D still creates a problematic arrangement.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist becomes burnt out and considers leaving the profession. From an ethical perspective, what action is appropriate?

options.A: Continue working until finding another job, regardless of ability to provide quality services
options.B: Recognize burnout affects competence and seek support through consultation, supervision, or professional development; consider temporary role adjustments or seeking help
options.C: Hide the burnout from employers and colleagues
options.D: Immediately resign without notice

questions.explanation: Self-care and addressing burnout are ethical obligations because they protect student safety and service quality. Option A risks harming students. Options C and D are ineffective or irresponsible.

questions.question_stem: An ethical decision-making model typically includes these steps:

options.A: Identify the dilemma, consider alternatives, consult when appropriate, decide, and document
options.B: Identify the problem, quickly choose the easiest option
options.C: Consult a lawyer before any decision
options.D: Make decisions alone to avoid involving others

questions.explanation: This systematic approach ensures ethical decisions are thoughtful and defensible. Option B skips careful consideration. Options C and D over-rely on single sources of guidance.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist learns that a student has made a threat of violence toward a peer. Regarding confidentiality:

options.A: The threat is confidential and should not be shared
options.B: The threat must be reported to administrators and parents; threat assessment is warranted to determine immediate risk
options.C: The threat should be reported only to the principal
options.D: The threat is a minor concern and can be addressed informally

questions.explanation: Duty to warn/protect requires reporting serious threats while conducting appropriate assessment. Option A fails the duty. Option C is incomplete. Option D underestimates risk.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist receives a request for educational records from a student's grandparent who does not have legal custody. The psychologist should:

options.A: Automatically refuse because the grandparent is not the custodial parent
options.B: Verify FERPA rights by confirming parental rights; generally, custodial parents have rights unless a court order specifies otherwise; provide records if the custodial parent consents
options.C: Provide records directly to the grandparent without verification
options.D: Provide records only with the student's consent, regardless of age

questions.explanation: FERPA rights follow legal custody unless a court order changes this. Option A is too rigid. Option C violates FERPA. Option D ignores parental rights.

questions.question_stem: Supervision of a school psychologist intern or trainee requires:

options.A: Allowing the trainee to practice independently with minimal oversight
options.B: Regular oversight, feedback, and assessment of competence; clear scope of practice; and ongoing communication about performance
options.C: Supervision only if problems arise
options.D: Delegating most work to the trainee to reduce the supervisor's caseload

questions.explanation: Supervision means actively overseeing competence and ensuring safe practice. Option A creates liability. Option C is reactive. Option D violates supervision responsibilities.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist is invited to serve on a committee that will affect their students' opportunities. When accepting this role, the psychologist should:

options.A: Decline to avoid any appearance of bias
options.B: Accept and work toward what's best for students while being transparent about the potential for conflict of interest
options.C: Accept and prioritize the committee's goals over individual student advocacy
options.D: Accept but avoid advocating for their students to appear unbiased

questions.explanation: School psychologists can serve in multiple roles; the key is transparency and managing conflicts appropriately. Option A unnecessarily limits participation. Options C and D either compromise students or abandon advocacy.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist receives a subpoena for a student's records in a custody case. The psychologist should:

options.A: Automatically comply with the subpoena
options.B: Notify the parents/guardians of the subpoena; provide records according to FERPA and state laws; consult with administration if needed
options.C: Refuse the subpoena entirely
options.D: Provide records without notifying the family

questions.explanation: Subpoenas require compliance but also notification to parents and adherence to FERPA protections. Option A skips notification. Option C may result in contempt. Option D violates notification requirements.

questions.question_stem: According to NASP ethics, when a school psychologist disagrees with a team decision they believe is harmful to a student, they should:

options.A: Go along with the decision to maintain team harmony
options.B: Advocate for the student's interests; if concerns persist after expressing them to the team, escalate through appropriate channels or document dissent
options.C: Immediately report the team to administration without team discussion
options.D: Resign from the case

questions.explanation:
Professional advocacy for students is an ethical obligation. Option A sacrifices student interest. Option C skips team problem-solving. Option D is extreme.

---

questions.question_stem: Professional malpractice in school psychology requires:

options.A: Only that the psychologist was wrong about something
options.B: A legal duty to the student, a breach of duty (not meeting standard of care), causation (breach caused harm), and actual damages
options.C: That the psychologist acted intentionally to harm the student
options.D: Liability for all student problems occurring during employment

questions.explanation: These four elements are required for a malpractice claim. Option A requires only error. Option C requires intent (separate from negligence). Option D creates unrealistic liability.

questions.question_stem: The standard of care in school psychology is defined as:

options.A: What a perfectly trained school psychologist would do
options.B: What a reasonably competent school psychologist with similar training would do in similar circumstances
options.C: Following the most advanced research-based practices
options.D: Whatever approach the individual psychologist chooses

questions.explanation: Standard of care is a reasonable professional standard, not perfection or the most advanced approach. Option A is too strict. Option C requires constant cutting-edge practice. Option D has no standard.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist's case notes serve what legal and ethical purpose?

options.A: They have no legal significance
options.B: They document the basis for decisions and recommendations, provide a record for continuity of care, and can defend the psychologist if practice is questioned
options.C: They are only for the psychologist's memory
options.D: They should be vague to protect privacy

questions.explanation: Documentation is critical for decision support and legal protection. Option A ignores legal importance. Option C is insufficient. Option D reduces usability.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist supervises a trainee. If the trainee makes an error that harms a student, who is liable?

options.A: Only the trainee
options.B: Only the supervisor
options.C: Both the trainee and supervisor, with liability proportional to each person's role and oversight
options.D: The school district but not the individuals

questions.explanation: Vicarious liability means supervisors are responsible for supervisees' work; liability can be shared. Option A absolves the supervisor. Option B over-represents the supervisor's responsibility. Option D doesn't account for individual professional responsibility.

questions.question_stem: A supervision model that matches challenge level and support to the supervisee's developmental stage is called:

options.A: Discrimination model
options.B: Developmental model
options.C: Competence-based model
options.D: Standard model

questions.explanation: The developmental model adjusts supervision to the supervisee's experience level. Option A is a different model. Options C and D are less specific terms.

questions.question_stem: Before delegating assessment responsibilities to a supervisee, the supervisor should:

options.A: Assume the supervisee can handle anything with their degree
options.B: Clarify the supervisee's competence, training, and experience; ensure the task is within their scope; provide appropriate oversight; and plan for review of work
options.C: Delegate without discussion to test the supervisee's abilities
options.D: Allow the supervisee to decide what they can handle

questions.explanation: Supervisors must assess competence and ensure appropriate delegation with oversight. Option A makes assumptions. Option C provides no support or direction. Option D abdicates supervisory responsibility.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist fails to conduct a triennial reevaluation on time for a student with an IEP. This represents:

options.A: A minor administrative oversight
options.B: A potential breach of IDEA and the standard of care that could result in liability
options.C: A problem only if someone complains
options.D: Normal practice since reevaluations are often delayed

questions.explanation: Timeliness of evaluations is required under IDEA and the standard of care; failure can harm the student and violate law. Option A minimizes the issue. Option C makes liability contingent on complaint rather than actual breach. Option D normalizes inadequate practice.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist conducts an evaluation and recommends a specific intervention. Later, the recommended intervention is not implemented, and the student's problems worsen. Is the psychologist liable?

options.A: Yes, always, because the student's condition worsened
options.B: Only if the recommendation was appropriate and reasonable; implementation is the school's responsibility
options.C: Not if the recommendation was documented
options.D: Only if the psychologist physically implements it

questions.explanation: The psychologist's liability is for providing appropriate recommendations; implementation is the school's responsibility. Option A creates liability for others' failures. Option C, while documentation helps, doesn't determine liability. Option D is too narrow.

questions.question_stem: When a supervisee makes an error that affects a student, the supervisor's appropriate response includes:

options.A: Immediately terminating the supervisee
options.B: Addressing the error, understanding how it occurred, ensuring harm is minimized, adjusting supervision/training, and documenting the incident
options.C: Pretending it didn't happen to avoid embarrassment
options.D: Blaming the supervisee publicly

questions.explanation: Responsible supervision includes addressing errors constructively, documenting, and preventing recurrence. Option A is excessive. Option C ignores ethical obligation. Option D is harmful.

questions.question_stem: School psychological records should typically be retained for:

options.A: The school year only
options.B: Until the student graduates or leaves the district; longer if required by law or if the student may return
options.C: Only as long as the school psychologist remembers them
options.D: Never in written form, only digital

questions.explanation: Records support continuity and protect the student legally. Option A is too short. Option C is unreliable. Option D ignores the value of backup documentation.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist uses digital communication to discuss a student case with a teacher via text message. Liability concerns include:

options.A: No concerns because text messages are informal
options.B: Text messages may lack confidentiality protections, create incomplete documentation, and be discoverable in legal proceedings
options.C: Only concerns if the message contains diagnostic information
options.D: Digital communication is always protected

questions.explanation: Electronic communication requires careful consideration of security and documentation. Option A minimizes risks. Option C is too narrow. Option D is false.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist discovers that assessment materials have been stored in a non-secure location where unauthorized people could access them. This represents:

options.A: A minor issue
options.B: A breach of confidentiality and standard of care; corrective action and potentially notification is warranted
options.C: A problem only if someone actually accessed the materials
options.D: A responsibility of the custodian, not the psychologist

questions.explanation: Security of materials is a professional and legal obligation; unauthorized access risk requires action. Option A minimizes the breach. Option C requires actual access to matter. Option D doesn't absolve the psychologist of responsibility.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist documents a student's behavior in clinical notes but uses derogatory language. This creates a liability risk because:

options.A: It has no legal significance
options.B: Derogatory language is subjective, could be seen as bias, and is unprofessional; the notes could be discovered and damage the psychologist's credibility and the school's defense
options.C: Only if the student sees it
options.D: All documentation must be positive regardless of facts

questions.explanation: Professional, objective documentation is legally and ethically important. Option A ignores legal implications. Option C limits concern to student access. Option D requires dishonest documentation.

questions.question_stem: Regarding FERPA and school psychological records, the record retention and confidentiality requirements apply:

options.A: Only to school psychologists, not administrators
options.B: To all staff handling student records; FERPA requires protected access and specific retention timelines
options.C: Only during the school year
options.D: To test scores but not clinical notes

questions.explanation: FERPA applies to all personnel with access to records. Options A, C, and D create false limitations.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist is asked to testify in court about a student's evaluation. Liability concerns include:

options.A: No concerns; they are obligated to testify
options.B: Ensuring the testimony is accurate, truthful, and limited to areas of expertise; preparing for cross-examination
options.C: Providing favorable testimony regardless of facts
options.D: Refusing to testify to avoid liability

questions.explanation: Expert testimony requires accuracy and honesty; preparation helps reduce liability. Option A assumes no concerns. Option C violates honesty. Option D may result in legal consequences for refusal.

questions.question_stem: A supervisor allows a supervisee to conduct high-stakes assessments without adequate training or oversight. If harm results, the supervisor's liability is:

options.A: Zero because the supervisee conducted the work
options.B: Substantial because the supervisor delegated beyond the supervisee's competence and failed to provide appropriate oversight
options.C: Only if the supervisor intended harm
options.D: Covered by the school district entirely

questions.explanation: Supervisors are responsible for ensuring supervisees operate within their competence with appropriate oversight. Option A avoids responsibility. Option C requires intent, not just negligence. Option D may protect the school but not the supervisor personally.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist provides a written evaluation report that is shown to the student's family. The report should:

options.A: Be written in technical jargon to establish authority
options.B: Use clear language, explain findings in understandable terms, and explain limitations and recommendations
options.C: Avoid explaining any negative findings to protect the family's feelings
options.D: Include raw test data without interpretation

questions.explanation: Clear, intelligible communication serves the family and protects the psychologist legally. Option A is unnecessarily complex. Option C withholds important information. Option D provides data without context.

questions.question_stem: Liability insurance for school psychologists typically covers:

options.A: All professional mistakes
options.B: Malpractice, professional liability, and defense costs, with specific exclusions for intentional harm or illegal acts
options.C: Everything the psychologist does
options.D: Nothing related to school practice

questions.explanation: Insurance covers professional negligence but excludes intentional harm and illegal conduct. Options A, C, and D misrepresent typical coverage.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist fails to report suspected child abuse, violating mandatory reporting laws. Liability could include:

options.A: No liability since school psychologists are not mandatory reporters
options.B: Criminal liability for failure to report, civil liability to the child, professional liability, and disciplinary action
options.C: Liability only if the abuse continues
options.D: Liability only if reported to the school board

questions.explanation: Mandatory reporters have legal and professional obligations; failure carries multiple consequences. Option A incorrectly states school psychologists aren't mandatory reporters. Option C requires future harm. Option D requires administrative reporting.

questions.question_stem: When a school psychologist believes they may have made an error that could harm a student, the appropriate liability-conscious action is to:

options.A: Hide the error hoping no one notices
options.B: Immediately disclose the error to supervisors, correct it, inform the family if appropriate, and document actions taken
options.C: Tell colleagues informally
options.D: Wait to see if harm actually results

questions.explanation:
Disclosure and correction minimize liability and protect the student. Option A increases liability through concealment. Option C creates informal discussions without proper documentation. Option D delays correction.

---

questions.question_stem: NASP's continuing education requirements for school psychologists serve to:

options.A: Generate revenue for NASP
options.B: Ensure that practitioners maintain current knowledge of research and practice standards
options.C: Create unnecessary paperwork
options.D: Replace supervision with education

questions.explanation: Continuing education maintains professional competence and addresses the ethical obligation to provide evidence-based practice. Options A, C, and D mischaracterize the purpose.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist is the only psychologist in a rural district with limited access to professional development opportunities. They should:

options.A: Accept limited competence as unavoidable in rural settings
options.B: Actively pursue professional development through professional organizations, online learning, consultation, and peer networks; advocate for resources to support ongoing learning
options.C: Focus only on current work and avoid extending into new areas
options.D: Assume rural students need different services than urban students and reduce expectations

questions.explanation: Lifelong learning is an ethical obligation regardless of setting. Options A and C abandon the obligation. Option D inappropriately reduces services.

questions.question_stem: Individual advocacy in school psychology means:

options.A: Only providing direct services to students
options.B: Promoting the interests and rights of individual students within the system (e.g., advocating for appropriate services, accommodations, or challenging inappropriate placement)
options.C: Avoiding conflict with school administrators
options.D: Only addressing academic issues

questions.explanation: Individual advocacy directly addresses student needs within the system. Option A is too narrow. Option C avoids advocacy when needed. Option D is too limited.

questions.question_stem: Systems advocacy in school psychology involves:

options.A: Working only with students one-on-one
options.B: Addressing policies, practices, and structures that affect groups of students; using data to influence policy and practice changes
options.C: Assuming individual services are sufficient
options.D: Working only at the district level, not in schools

questions.explanation: Systems advocacy addresses broader structures affecting populations. Option A is individual advocacy. Option C limits advocacy scope. Option D restricts where advocacy occurs.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist becomes aware that the discipline rate for students of color is disproportionately high. From a professional growth and advocacy perspective, they should:

options.A: Accept this as a reality and move on
options.B: Engage in professional learning about bias and disproportionality; collaborate with colleagues to examine the issue; advocate for policy and practice changes based on data
options.C: Blame teachers for bias
options.D: Only address individual cases without examining systemic issues

questions.explanation: Professional growth includes learning about systemic issues and engaging in advocacy and change work. Option A abandons responsibility. Option C is reductive. Option D ignores systems-level action.

questions.question_stem: NASP position statements serve what purpose in professional practice?

options.A: They are optional opinions
options.B: They provide guidance based on research and professional consensus on issues affecting school psychological practice
options.C: They are only for beginning practitioners
options.D: They are rarely relevant to individual practice decisions

questions.explanation: Position statements guide practice on important topics. Option A minimizes their significance. Option C limits their application. Option D ignores their relevance.

questions.question_stem: Professional mentoring and supervision relationships contribute to professional growth by:

options.A: Creating dependency on the mentor/supervisor
options.B: Providing experienced guidance, feedback on practice, modeling professional behavior, and reflective discussion
options.C: Reducing the need for independent thinking
options.D: Primarily evaluating performance only

questions.explanation: Effective mentoring/supervision supports growth through multiple mechanisms. Option A creates unhealthy dependency. Option C undermines independence. Option D is too narrow.

questions.question_stem: Self-care and preventing burnout are ethical obligations because:

options.A: They are personal preferences
options.B: Burnout affects professional competence and the quality of services to students
options.C: They are unrelated to professional practice
options.D: Only administrators should address burnout

questions.explanation: Self-care directly affects professional quality and student safety. Option A minimizes its importance. Option C denies the connection. Option D inappropriately defers responsibility.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist is invited to present at a conference on culturally responsive intervention. This opportunity supports professional growth by:

options.A: Adding to resume credentials only
options.B: Requiring advanced knowledge, integrating recent research, connecting with colleagues, and modeling professional contribution to the field
options.C: Creating an unnecessary burden
options.D: Being relevant only if it's a paid position

questions.explanation: Presenting integrates learning, models professional behavior, and contributes to the field. Option A reduces the benefit. Option C dismisses the opportunity. Option D makes payment the criterion.

questions.question_stem: Advocacy at the policy level might include:

options.A: Only working with individual students
options.B: Providing data to policymakers, serving on committees, communicating with legislators, or contributing to policy recommendations that affect educational equity
options.C: Assuming policymakers won't listen to school psychologists
options.D: Only following policies without question

questions.explanation: Policy advocacy uses professional expertise to influence systems affecting students. Option A is individual advocacy. Option C assumes ineffectiveness. Option D abandons advocacy.

questions.question_stem: Evidence-based practice as an ethical obligation means:

options.A: Using only the newest published research
options.B: Using practices supported by research evidence, integrating professional judgment and client values, and updating knowledge continuously
options.C: Avoiding practices not yet validated
options.D: Implementing practices identically across all students

questions.explanation: EBP balances research with professional judgment and individual context. Option A requires only the newest studies. Option C is too rigid. Option D ignores individualization.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist joins a professional organization and becomes active in committees. This contributes to professional growth through:

options.A: Primarily networking benefits
options.B: Access to current research, collaboration on important issues, leadership opportunities, and contributing to the profession
options.C: Unnecessary time commitment
options.D: Only if they become president

questions.explanation: Professional organization involvement supports multiple growth dimensions. Option A is too narrow. Option C dismisses value. Option D overstates requirements.

questions.question_stem: Using school data to drive change (data advocacy) involves:

options.A: Collecting data only for compliance purposes
options.B: Analyzing available data to identify disparities, needs, or areas for improvement; presenting findings to stakeholders; advocating for changes based on evidence
options.C: Keeping data private to avoid criticism
options.D: Assuming data cannot influence policy

questions.explanation: Data advocacy systematically uses evidence to guide change. Option A limits data use. Option C withholds important information. Option D underestimates data influence.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist realizes they lack competence in a new area their district is emphasizing (e.g., suicide prevention). The professional growth obligation includes:

options.A: Continuing without training to avoid admitting a gap
options.B: Seeking training, consultation, and supervision; admitting the limitation while working to address it
options.C: Refusing to work in the area
options.D: Relying on colleagues' knowledge without learning

questions.explanation: Professional growth involves recognizing gaps and addressing them. Option A maintains unsafe practice. Option C may be necessary if gaps are substantial, but seeking training is preferred. Option D limits personal competence.

questions.question_stem: Contributing to research knowledge as a professional growth and advocacy goal might involve:

options.A: Only for researchers in universities
options.B: Collecting and analyzing data from practice, participating in research, or publishing findings that inform the field
options.C: Only reading research, not conducting it
options.D: Assuming school-based data is not valuable for research

questions.explanation: School psychologists contribute valuable practice-based knowledge. Option A restricts research to academics. Option C limits engagement to consumption. Option D underestimates school-based data.

questions.question_stem: Cultural responsiveness as an ongoing learning obligation means:

options.A: Taking one diversity training and considering the obligation met
options.B: Continuously examining one's own biases, learning about diverse cultures, adjusting practice, and reflecting on effectiveness with different populations
options.C: Assuming cultural knowledge is static
options.D: Only relevant for school psychologists from diverse backgrounds

questions.explanation: Cultural humility is ongoing; it requires continuous learning and self-reflection. Option A assumes one-time learning is sufficient. Option C ignores cultural change. Option D inappropriately limits who should engage.

questions.question_stem: Peer consultation as a professional development practice involves:

options.A: Isolated individual practice
options.B: Discussing cases and dilemmas with trusted colleagues to gain perspectives, reduce isolation, and refine decision-making C) Only seeking supervision when required
options.D: Depending entirely on peer input for final decisions  **Correct Answer: B**  **Explanation:** Peer consultation provides collegial support and diverse perspectives. Option A avoids collaborative learning. Option C limits consultation. Option D abdicates personal responsibility.

questions.explanation: Peer consultation provides collegial support and diverse perspectives. Option A avoids collaborative learning. Option C limits consultation. Option D abdicates personal responsibility.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist engages in reflective practice by:

options.A: Never questioning their own work
options.B: Regularly examining their decisions, outcomes, and biases; considering what worked and what didn't; adjusting future practice based on reflection
options.C: Only reflecting when things go wrong
options.D: Assuming their approach is always correct

questions.explanation: Reflective practice is intentional examination of practice to improve. Option A avoids improvement. Option C is reactive. Option D prevents growth.

questions.question_stem: The distinction between professional growth for compliance (meeting CEU requirements) and professional growth for competence is:

options.A: There is no difference
options.B: Compliance fulfills minimum requirements; true competence growth requires engaging with material deeply, applying learning, and changing practice
options.C: Compliance is more important than competence
options.D: They are unrelated

questions.explanation: True professional development changes practice and competence. Option A conflates minimal and optimal growth. Option C reverses priorities. Option D ignores the relationship.

questions.question_stem: When a school psychologist becomes aware of emerging issues affecting students (e.g., pandemic effects, social media impacts), the professional development obligation includes:

options.A: Assuming the issues are temporary and requiring no learning
options.B: Seeking knowledge about the issues, understanding their impact on students, developing competence in addressing them, and advocating for appropriate services
options.C: Waiting for others to become experts first
options.D: Limiting practice to traditional areas unaffected by emerging issues

questions.explanation:
Professional growth addresses emerging challenges affecting students. Option A minimizes current issues. Option C delays necessary learning. Option D creates outdated practice.

---

questions.question_stem: The Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) applies to:

options.A: All schools regardless of funding source
options.B: Schools that receive federal education funding
options.C: Only private schools
options.D: Only schools with large student populations

questions.explanation: FERPA applies to schools receiving federal funding (most public and many private schools). Option A is too broad. Options C and D wrongly limit applicability.

questions.question_stem: An "educational record" under FERPA includes:

options.A: Only test scores and grades
options.B: Records directly related to a student in the school's possession (including grades, test scores, assessments, disciplinary records, and notes)
options.C: Only documents created by teachers
options.D: Records about students' family members

questions.explanation: Educational records are broadly defined as records about the student created or maintained by the school. Options A and C are too narrow. Option D is not about the student.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist keeps a personal case note that is used only to remember details for the next session but is never shared. Does this "sole possession note" constitute an educational record under FERPA?

options.A: Yes, all notes are educational records
options.B: No, sole possession notes created solely as a memory aid and not shared are exempt from FERPA
options.C: Only if they contain test scores
options.D: Only if they mention the student's disability

questions.explanation: The sole possession note exception applies to personal memory aids not shared. Option A over-includes. Options C and D create false criteria.

questions.question_stem: A student's parent requests access to the student's educational records. The school should:

options.A: Automatically deny the request to protect the student
options.B: Grant access within a reasonable time; provide records that the school maintains about the student
options.C: Allow access only if the student is present
options.D: Provide the records but only to the student's attorney

questions.explanation: FERPA grants parents the right to inspect educational records. Option A denies a legal right. Option C creates unnecessary conditions. Option D inappropriately limits access.

questions.question_stem: A parent requests to amend their child's educational record because they believe information is inaccurate. The procedure is:

options.A: The school can deny the request if it disagrees
options.B: The school must consider the request; if denied, the parent has the right to a hearing and to place a statement in the record
options.C: The school must automatically agree
options.D: Only the student can request amendment

questions.explanation: FERPA provides an amendment right with procedural protections including a hearing if the school denies the request. Option A wrongly permits denial without process. Option C assumes automatic approval. Option D ignores parent rights.

questions.question_stem: A student is 18 years old and a high school senior. When the student reaches age 18, the right to access educational records transfers to:

options.A: Automatically transfers to the student
options.B: Remains with the parent unless the student is emancipated or a court order specifies otherwise; the parent may retain access
options.C: Transfers to the school
options.D: Transfers to the district superintendent

questions.explanation: Age 18 transfers rights to the student, but parents retain rights unless the student is emancipated or court-ordered otherwise. Option A ignores continued parental rights. Options C and D are incorrect.

questions.question_stem: A school may share a student's educational records without parental consent to school personnel who have a:

options.A: Desire to know about the student
options.B: Legitimate educational interest (need to know information to perform job-related duties for the student)
options.C: General interest in the student
options.D: Request for information

questions.explanation: The legitimate educational interest exception permits sharing with staff who need information for education-related purposes. Options A, C, and D create unjustified exceptions.

questions.question_stem: A school may disclose educational records without parental consent in a health emergency if:

options.A: Any parent or staff member thinks there is an emergency
options.B: There is a legitimate health/safety emergency requiring immediate knowledge of information in the records
options.C: The student asks for the information
options.D: The school's attorney recommends it

questions.explanation: The health/safety emergency exception applies to serious, immediate situations. Option A lowers the threshold inappropriately. Option C is not an emergency criterion. Option D doesn't create an exception.

questions.question_stem: Directory information under FERPA includes information that schools may disclose without consent unless the parent opts out. Typical directory information includes:

options.A: Test scores and grades
options.B: Student name, address, phone number, date of birth, and participation in activities
options.C: Disciplinary records and special education status
options.D: Social security number

questions.explanation: Directory information typically includes basic identifying information and activity participation. Options A and C are sensitive information, not directory information. Option D is extremely sensitive.

questions.question_stem: A school receives a judicial order (subpoena or court order) to produce a student's educational records. The school should:

options.A: Automatically comply without notifying the parent
options.B: Verify the judicial order is legitimate, notify the parent (unless the order directs otherwise), and produce the records
options.C: Refuse the judicial order to protect privacy
options.D: Share the records only with the school board

questions.explanation: Schools must comply with legitimate judicial orders while generally notifying parents. Option A skips notification. Option C may result in contempt. Option D incorrectly limits who receives the records.

questions.question_stem: The distinction between FERPA and HIPAA is:

options.A: They are the same law
options.B: FERPA governs student records in schools; HIPAA governs health records in medical settings; they apply differently to school psychological records
options.C: HIPAA is more protective than FERPA
options.D: Only HIPAA applies to schools

questions.explanation: They are separate laws with different applicability. Option A conflates them. Option C makes an inappropriate comparison. Option D ignores FERPA's application.

questions.question_stem: A noncustodial parent requests the student's school records. Under FERPA:

options.A: The noncustodial parent has no rights
options.B: The noncustodial parent generally has FERPA rights unless a court order specifies otherwise (e.g., no contact order)
options.C: Only the custodial parent has rights
options.D: Both parents must request together

questions.explanation: FERPA doesn't automatically deny noncustodial parents; court orders determine rights. Option A wrongly denies all noncustodial parent rights. Option C oversimplifies. Option D creates unnecessary conditions.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist is asked by a researcher to provide data from student records for research. The school should:

options.A: Automatically refuse all research requests
options.B: Require informed consent from parents; de-identify data appropriately; ensure the research has proper ethical review; and clarify any restrictions on data use
options.C: Provide the data if the research is important
options.D: Allow the researcher to access records directly

questions.explanation: Schools can share data for research with proper protections and consent. Option A unnecessarily restricts research. Option C lacks procedural protection. Option D violates confidentiality.

questions.question_stem: Personally identifiable information (PII) in educational records includes:

options.A: Only social security numbers
options.B: Information that can identify the student (name, ID number, address, parent information, biometric records)
options.C: Only test scores
options.D: Information visible in the school yearbook

questions.explanation: PII is broadly defined as any information identifying the student. Option A is too narrow. Option C is not PII by itself. Option D may be PII depending on context.

questions.question_stem: A school is transferring a student's records to a new school the student is attending. The school should:

options.A: Send all records without limitation
options.B: Notify the parent of the transfer (if required); send relevant records with appropriate controls; track disclosure
options.C: Ask the parent for permission before transferring
options.D: Keep the records instead of transferring them

questions.explanation: Schools routinely transfer records to new schools with notification and tracking. Option A doesn't include notification. Option C creates unnecessary delay. Option D prevents the new school from accessing necessary information.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist may share de-identified school data (with personal identifiers removed) for research or evaluation without parental consent if:

options.A: The data is published somewhere
options.B: The data is properly de-identified to make re-identification very difficult and is used for legitimate research/evaluation purposes
options.C: The data is only summarized
options.D: Any person requests it

questions.explanation: De-identified data can be shared if truly de-identified and used appropriately. Option A is not a criterion. Option C doesn't ensure de-identification. Option D permits inappropriate access.

questions.question_stem: When a school provides a parent an annual notification of FERPA rights, the notification should include:

options.A: Only the names of staff who access records
options.B: The parents' rights to inspect, amend, and control disclosure; the process to exercise rights; and exceptions to consent requirements
options.C: Only technical information about record storage
options.D: Information about other students' records

questions.explanation: The annual notice informs parents of their substantive rights. Option A is too limited. Option C is administrative detail. Option D violates others' privacy.

questions.question_stem: A student's parent is incarcerated and requests records about the student. Under FERPA:

options.A: Access is automatically denied due to incarceration
options.B: The parent's incarceration status doesn't remove FERPA rights unless a court order restricts contact
options.C: The school must consult law enforcement first
options.D: Only the student can receive the records

questions.explanation: FERPA rights aren't forfeited by incarceration; a court order would restrict rights. Option A inappropriately uses incarceration as grounds for denial. Option C isn't required. Option D limits access.

questions.question_stem: A school psychologist provides a clinical interpretation to a teacher verbally about a student's assessment results. Does this violate FERPA?

options.A: Yes, any sharing of records violates FERPA
options.B: No, if the teacher has a legitimate educational interest; however, the psychologist should document what was shared and with whom
options.C: Only if the discussion happens in a hallway
options.D: Yes, because it's verbal rather than written

questions.explanation: Sharing with staff with legitimate educational interest doesn't violate FERPA; documentation supports compliance. Option A over-restricts appropriate sharing. Option C makes location the criterion. Option D makes medium the issue.

questions.question_stem: A student's family expresses concern that their child's records may have been accessed without authorization. The school should:

options.A: Automatically deny the concern
options.B: Investigate the access logs, verify who accessed the records and when, address any unauthorized access, and inform the family of findings
options.C: Promise to increase security without investigating
options.D: Tell the family there's nothing they can do

questions.explanation:
Schools should investigate potential unauthorized access and inform the family. Option A dismisses concerns. Option C takes action without investigation. Option D denies responsibility.

---

questions.question_stem: The six core principles of IDEA are:

options.A: Assessment, IEP, services, placement, funding, and documentation
options.B: Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE), Least Restrictive Environment (LRE), nondiscriminatory evaluation, appropriate evaluation, parent participation, and procedural safeguards
options.C: Testing, intervention, consultation, communication, and accountability
options.D: Special education, regular education, technology, transportation, mental health, and career services

questions.explanation: These are IDEA's foundational principles that guide all special education practice. Options A, C, and D include important but not the six core principles.

questions.question_stem: FAPE under IDEA means:

options.A: Education at no cost in the regular classroom
options.B: Special education and related services at public expense, meeting state standards, and provided in accordance with an appropriate IEP
options.C: Any education the school can afford to provide
options.D: Education only for students with significant disabilities

questions.explanation: FAPE is comprehensive special education at public cost with an appropriate IEP. Option A incorrectly limits to regular classroom. Option C allows insufficient standards. Option D inappropriately restricts eligibility.

questions.question_stem: The 13 disability categories under IDEA include all EXCEPT:

options.A: Emotional Disturbance
options.B: Autism
options.C: Learning disability
options.D: Environmental disadvantage

questions.explanation: IDEA defines 13 categories; environmental disadvantage (low SES or poverty alone) is not a category. Options A, B, and C are valid categories.

questions.question_stem: An IEP (Individualized Education Program) must include:

options.A: Only the student's test scores
options.B: Present levels of performance, annual goals, services, accommodations, placement, and progress monitoring; evaluation timeline; and measurable goals
options.C: The student's entire file
options.D: Goals chosen by the district alone

questions.explanation: IEPs require specific components addressing the student's current performance, goals, services, and monitoring. Option A is too limited. Option C includes extraneous information. Option D violates parent participation.

questions.question_stem: The timeline for conducting a comprehensive initial evaluation under IDEA is:

options.A: Within 30 days of referral
options.B: Within 60 days of parental consent (or the state's established timeline)
options.C: Within 1 year
options.D: Within 6 months

questions.explanation: IDEA requires evaluation within 60 days (or state timeline) of parental consent. Options A, C, and D are incorrect timeframes.

questions.question_stem: A Manifestation Determination Review (MDR) must be conducted when:

options.A: A student is first referred for special education
options.B: A student with a disability will be removed from placement for more than 10 consecutive school days or has a pattern of removals totaling 10 days
options.C: A student's test scores change significantly
options.D: Every year at the annual IEP meeting

questions.explanation: MDR determines whether behavior resulting in removal is related to the disability. Option A is inappropriate timing. Option C doesn't trigger MDR. Option D isn't the correct standard.

questions.question_stem: Under IDEA's disciplinary provisions, if a student with a disability is suspended for more than 10 days in a school year:

options.A: No notice is required
options.B: An MDR is required to determine if the behavior is related to the disability; if related, different procedures apply
options.C: The student can be expelled
options.D: The parent must agree to the suspension

questions.explanation: Extended removals trigger MDR and procedural protections. Option A skips required procedures. Option C is incorrect; expulsion requires special procedures. Option D isn't the standard.

questions.question_stem: The stay-put provision under IDEA means:

options.A: A student must remain in school regardless of behavior
options.B: During due process proceedings, the student stays in the current educational placement unless the parent and school agree otherwise, unless there is danger or drugs/weapons
options.C: A student cannot be moved to a more restrictive setting
options.D: Parents can prevent any change in placement

questions.explanation: Stay-put protects the current placement during disputes, with limited exceptions. Option A over-simplifies. Option C is too restrictive. Option D doesn't capture the legal standard.

questions.question_stem: A parent disagrees with a school's special education decisions. Under IDEA, the parent may request:

options.A: Only a meeting with the principal
options.B: Mediation or a due process hearing to challenge the school's decision
options.C: Help from the school's attorney
options.D: A student vote on the decision

questions.explanation: IDEA provides mediation and due process hearing rights. Option A provides no formal redress. Option C is not a parent right. Option D is inappropriate.

questions.question_stem: IDEA Part B serves students ages:

options.A: Birth to 3
options.B: 3 to 5
options.C: 3 to 21
options.D: All ages

questions.explanation: IDEA Part B covers school-age students aged 3-21. Option A is Part C (Early Intervention). Option B is Part B for preschool but not the full range. Option D is incorrect.

questions.question_stem: Related services under IDEA include:

options.A: Only special education instruction
options.B: Services needed for the student to benefit from special education (speech therapy, occupational therapy, counseling, transportation)
options.C: Only services available in general education
options.D: Services chosen by the student alone

questions.explanation: Related services support the student's access to special education. Option A is too narrow. Option C conflates general and special services. Option D doesn't involve appropriate team decision-making.

questions.question_stem: When considering Least Restrictive Environment (LRE), schools must:

options.A: Place all students with disabilities in general education
options.B: Consider a continuum of placements; move to more restrictive settings only if supplementary aids and services cannot meet the student's needs in a less restrictive setting
options.C: Place students in the most restrictive available setting
options.D: Decide placement based on the disability category alone

questions.explanation: LRE requires considering general education first with supports before moving to segregated settings. Option A is too absolute. Option C is opposite LRE. Option D is individualized.

questions.question_stem: A student is suspected of having a learning disability. Before evaluation, the school should:

options.A: Automatically refer for special education evaluation
options.B: Implement evidence-based interventions with progress monitoring; document RTI/MTSS data before evaluation
options.C: Tell the family the student definitely has a learning disability
options.D: Begin special education placement immediately

questions.explanation: IDEA emphasizes determining whether the student is a non-responder to quality instruction (RTI framework). Option A skips intervention. Option C is premature. Option D skips evaluation.

questions.question_stem: An IEP's annual goals should be:

options.A: Vague to allow flexibility
options.B: Measurable and specific; focused on improving the student's functioning in areas of identified need
options.C: Identical across all students
options.D: Set by the school without input

questions.explanation: Goals must be measurable to determine progress. Option A prevents measuring progress. Option C ignores individualization. Option D violates parent participation.

questions.question_stem: The nondiscriminatory evaluation requirement under IDEA means:

options.A: Evaluations should use only one assessment tool
options.B: Assessment must be in the student's native language when possible, use multiple measures, and avoid cultural bias; should be nonbiased and appropriate for the student
options.C: All students should receive identical assessments
options.D: Evaluation is not necessary if the student is failing

questions.explanation: Nondiscriminatory evaluation uses varied, unbiased, culturally responsive assessment. Option A is too narrow. Option C ignores individualization and cultural responsiveness. Option D contradicts evaluation obligations.

questions.question_stem: Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) rights under IDEA allow:

options.A: School personnel only to conduct evaluations
options.B: Parents to request an outside evaluation at school expense if they disagree with the school's evaluation; the school may challenge this request through due process
options.C: Only private evaluations
options.D: Parents to conduct any evaluation they want free of charge

questions.explanation: IEE provides a mechanism for independent assessment with due process safeguards. Option A prevents outside evaluation. Option C creates false dichotomy. Option D lacks the dispute mechanism.

questions.question_stem: A student with a disability requires an interpreter for school access. This would be provided as:

options.A: An optional service
options.B: A related service if necessary for the student to benefit from special education
options.C: Only during special education classes
options.D: Never, because accommodations are the student's responsibility

questions.explanation: Necessary services to access education are provided. Option A minimizes need. Option C limits inappropriate. Option D denies school responsibility.

questions.question_stem: Extended School Year (ESY) services should be provided to a student if:

options.A: The student requests them
options.B: The student is at risk of significant regression over the break without services
options.C: The district is required to provide them to all students
options.D: The parent requests them

questions.explanation: ESY is based on individual need for continuity; regression is the key criterion. Option A makes student choice the standard. Option C requires universal provision. Option D makes parent request alone sufficient.

questions.question_stem: Child Find under IDEA requires schools to:

options.A: Test all students for disabilities
options.B: Identify, locate, and evaluate all children with disabilities residing in the district, including homeless children and those in private schools
options.C: Only serve students who self-identify
options.D: Only evaluate students referred by parents

questions.explanation: Child Find is a comprehensive identification obligation. Option A tests all unnecessarily. Option C allows students to be missed. Option D limits referral sources.

questions.question_stem: Transition planning for a student with a disability should:

options.A: Begin at age 14 or earlier
options.B: Include goals related to post-secondary education, employment, and independent living; appropriate services; and student input
options.C: Only address special education services
options.D: Not involve the student

questions.explanation: Transition planning prepares for post-secondary life with student involvement. Option A is correct timing per IDEA. Option C ignores comprehensive transition. Option D excludes the student.

questions.question_stem: A 10-year-old student with severe asthma experiences frequent hospitalizations and uses an inhaler daily, which substantially limits her ability to participate in physical education and field trips. She does not qualify for services under IDEA. What is the most appropriate response?

options.A: The school has no obligation to provide accommodations unless she qualifies for an IEP.
options.B: She is eligible for a Section 504 plan that requires the school to provide necessary accommodations.
options.C: She should be placed in a separate educational setting to protect her health.
options.D: The school should only provide accommodations if the parents can pay for them.

questions.explanation: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act protects students with physical or mental impairments that substantially limit a major life activity—even if they don't qualify for IDEA services. This student's asthma substantially limits respiratory function and major life activities (breathing, physical activity). A 504 plan is required to provide reasonable accommodations. Option A is incorrect because Section 504 creates affirmative obligations for schools to provide accommodations regardless of IDEA eligibility. Option C is incorrect because segregation contradicts the least restrictive environment principle. Option D is incorrect because public schools must provide accommodations at no cost to families under Section 504.

questions.question_stem: A high school student with diabetes qualifies for accommodations under Section 504 but not IDEA. Which of the following BEST describes the primary difference in how services are provided between the two frameworks?

options.A: Section 504 students receive individualized instruction while IDEA students receive only accommodations.
options.B: IDEA provides specialized instruction and related services with federal funding; Section 504 provides accommodations to ensure access without specialized instruction or federal funding.
options.C: Section 504 is more restrictive and requires more frequent reevaluations than IDEA.
options.D: IDEA and Section 504 provide identical services but different funding mechanisms.

questions.explanation: The fundamental distinction is that IDEA requires specialized instruction and related services (which may require federal funds), whereas Section 504 requires accommodations—modifications to the environment or program that allow access without fundamentally altering the service. This student with diabetes might receive a 504 accommodation to test blood glucose in class or take breaks for insulin injections, but would not necessarily receive specialized instruction. Option A reverses the typical pattern. Option C is incorrect; Section 504 is actually broader and less restrictive in eligibility. Option D is false because they provide different types of services, not just different funding.

questions.question_stem: A student was diagnosed with seasonal allergies that affect concentration during spring months. According to the ADA Amendments Act of 2008, should this student be eligible for Section 504 protections?

options.A: Yes, because any allergy diagnosis automatically qualifies under Section 504.
options.B: Yes, if the allergies substantially limit a major life activity like learning or concentrating, even if the limitation is temporary.
options.C: No, because temporary conditions are explicitly excluded from Section 504 coverage.
options.D: No, because allergies are minor medical conditions not covered by ADA.

questions.explanation: The ADAAA of 2008 expanded the definition of "substantially limits" and made clear that conditions need not be permanent to qualify—temporary but significant impairments count if they substantially limit a major life activity. If this student's seasonal allergies substantially impair the ability to concentrate or learn during certain periods, they meet eligibility criteria. Option A is incorrect because diagnosis alone is insufficient; the impairment must substantially limit a major life activity. Option C contradicts ADAAA guidance. Option D is incorrect because the severity or "seriousness" of the condition is not the standard; rather, the degree of limitation of major life activities is what matters.

questions.question_stem: A school district evaluates a student for Section 504 eligibility and determines that she has ADHD. During the evaluation, the team should specifically assess which of the following?

options.A: Only the student's IQ and academic achievement scores to determine service level.
options.B: Whether the ADHD substantially limits one or more major life activities compared to the general population.
options.C: The medical diagnosis alone, without regard to functional impact.
options.D: Whether the student qualifies for IDEA services first before considering 504.

questions.explanation: Section 504 eligibility requires a functional analysis: does the disability substantially limit major life activities such as learning, attending, concentrating, or behaving? The determination is comparative—how does this student's functioning compare to the general population? Option A is incorrect because test scores alone don't establish "substantial limitation." Option C is incorrect because medical diagnosis is necessary but not sufficient; functional impact in major life activities is the critical factor. Option D is incorrect; 504 and IDEA eligibility are independent inquiries; a student can qualify for 504 without IDEA eligibility.

questions.question_stem: Who bears the primary responsibility for developing and monitoring a Section 504 plan?

options.A: The school psychologist, who should make all decisions without parent input.
options.B: The special education director, in consultation only with the student's teachers.
options.C: A 504 coordinator or team that includes the parent, relevant school staff, and may include the student, meeting the child's needs based on Section 504 eligibility criteria.
options.D: The student's general education teacher, working alone.

questions.explanation: Section 504 requires a collaborative team approach that includes the parent/guardian, school staff with knowledge of the child, and often the student themselves. The 504 coordinator facilitates this process. Option A is incorrect because school psychologists are team members but not sole decision-makers, and parent input is required by Section 504 procedural safeguards. Option B is incorrect because special education directors may be involved but the team should include relevant staff and parents. Option D is incorrect because a general education teacher alone lacks the authority and breadth of perspective required.

questions.question_stem: A student has a 504 plan that provides extended time on tests and a quiet testing location. A new teacher argues that these accommodations are "unfair" to other students. What should the school psychologist explain?

options.A: The teacher is correct; accommodations should only be provided to students with IEPs.
options.B: Accommodations are not advantages but rather modifications that level the playing field for students with disabilities, removing barriers to demonstrating knowledge—they are legally required and educationally appropriate.
options.C: Accommodations will be phased out as the student "catches up" academically.
options.D: The school can choose to ignore the 504 plan if teachers object.

questions.explanation: Accommodations address barriers created by disabilities; they do not provide unfair advantage. Extended time addresses processing or attention limitations, not intelligence. Providing the same test in the same conditions when a student has a documented limitation that affects test-taking is what creates unfairness. Option A is incorrect because 504 accommodations are equally valid and legally required. Option C is incorrect because accommodations address disabilities, which don't "resolve" with academic progress. Option D is incorrect because 504 plans are legally binding; schools cannot unilaterally disregard them.

questions.question_stem: Under ADA, a student with a history of cancer in remission (no current medical treatment) is considered to have a disability for purposes of eligibility if:

options.A: The cancer might recur in the future.
options.B: The history of cancer substantially limited major life activities, even if currently in remission.
options.C: The school district believes the student might become ill.
